questions.question: Define Minimum Spanning Tree (MST) and state any two of its properties.
keyPoints: MST is a spanning tree with minimum total edge weight
Properties: n-1 edges for n vertices, connected and acyclic
Cut Property: minimum edge crossing any cut belongs to some MST
Cycle Property: maximum edge in a cycle cannot be in any MST

questions.question: Explain the Cut Property used in MST algorithms.
keyPoints: Cut is a partition of vertices into two non-empty sets
For any cut, the minimum weight edge crossing it belongs to some MST
This is the foundation of greedy choice in Prim's and Kruskal's
Proof uses exchange argument: any spanning tree not containing this edge can be improved

questions.question: Describe Kruskal's algorithm for finding MST. Also mention its time complexity.
keyPoints: Sort all edges in ascending order of weight
Initialize each vertex as a separate component
For each edge in order: if it connects two different components, add edge and union them
Skip edges that would create cycles
Time complexity: O(E log V) using Union-Find

questions.question: Describe Prim's algorithm for finding MST. Also mention its time complexity.
keyPoints: Start from an arbitrary vertex, initialize key values to infinity
Maintain a priority queue of vertices not in MST
Repeatedly extract vertex with minimum key value
For each extracted vertex, update keys of adjacent vertices if edge weight is smaller
Time complexity: O(E log V) with binary heap, O(V²) with array

questions.question: What is Union-Find data structure? How is it used in Kruskal's algorithm?
keyPoints: Union-Find (Disjoint Set Union) tracks partitioned elements into disjoint sets
Supports Find: determines which set an element belongs to
Supports Union: merges two sets into one
In Kruskal's: Find checks if adding edge creates cycle, Union merges components
Path compression and union by rank make operations nearly O(1) amortized

questions.question: Differentiate between Kruskal's and Prim's algorithms for MST.
keyPoints: Kruskal's is edge-based, Prim's is vertex-based
Kruskal's processes edges in sorted order, Prim's grows one tree
Kruskal's works well on sparse graphs, Prim's on dense graphs
Both use greedy approach and produce same optimal MST
Kruskal's uses Union-Find, Prim's uses priority queue

questions.question: Under what condition is the MST unique? Explain with reasoning.
keyPoints: MST is unique when all edge weights are distinct
If multiple edges have same weight, different MSTs with same weight possible
Proof: Distinct weights create unique ordering, so only one MST results
If weights equal, different choices of equal-weight edges possible

questions.question: Trace through Kruskal's algorithm step by step on the following graph with vertices {A,B,C,D,E} and edges: AB(4), AC(3), AD(1), BC(2), BD(5), CD(4), DE(6), CE(7). Show how MST is constructed.
keyPoints: Sort edges: AD(1), BC(2), AC(3), AB(4), CD(4), BD(5), DE(6), CE(7)
Start: each vertex separate component
Add AD(1): connects A-D, components: {A,D}, {B}, {C}, {E}
Add BC(2): connects B-C, components: {A,D}, {B,C}, {E}
Add AC(3): A and C in different components, add edge, merge {A,D} with {B,C}
Add AB(4): A and B now in same component, skip (cycle)
Add CD(4): C and D in same component, skip
Add BD(5): B and D in same component, skip
MST edges: AD, BC, AC with total weight 1+2+3=6

questions.question: Trace through Prim's algorithm starting from vertex A on the same graph: vertices {A,B,C,D,E} with edges AB(4), AC(3), AD(1), BC(2), BD(5), CD(4), DE(6), CE(7). Show step-by-step construction of MST.
keyPoints: Start at A: key values - A=0, B=4, C=3, D=1, E=∞
Pick D (key 1): add edge AD(1). Update: B=4 (min), C=3 (unchanged), E=∞
Pick C (key 3): add edge AC(3). Update: B=2 (via C), D=1, E=∞
Pick B (key 2): add edge BC(2). Update: D=1, E=∞
Pick E (key ∞): No edge from tree to E, cannot proceed - graph may be disconnected
Actually for connected graph, need edge from any tree vertex to E - none exists, so E unreachable
Result: MST edges AD, AC, BC with total weight 1+3+2=6
Shows Prim's produces same total weight as Kruskal's

questions.question: Prove the Cut Property: For any cut in a graph, the minimum weight edge crossing the cut belongs to some Minimum Spanning Tree.
keyPoints: Let cut partition vertices into S and V-S
Let e be minimum weight edge crossing cut
Assume for contradiction: some MST T does not contain e
If T doesn't contain e, adding e to T creates a cycle
This cycle must have another edge e' crossing the cut (since e crosses cut)
Since e is minimum, weight(e) ≤ weight(e')
Replace e' with e in T: still spanning tree, weight not increased
New tree is also MST containing e
Therefore minimum edge crossing any cut belongs to some MST

questions.question: Write pseudocode for Kruskal's algorithm and analyze its time and space complexity.
keyPoints: SORT all edges by weight
FOR each vertex v: MAKE-SET(v)
FOR each edge (u,v) in sorted order:
IF FIND-SET(u) ≠ FIND-SET(v):
ADD edge (u,v) to MST
UNION(u,v)
Return MST
Time: O(E log E) = O(E log V) for sorting, O(E α(V)) for Union-Find ≈ O(E)
Space: O(V + E) for graph and Union-Find structures
Works best for sparse graphs where E ≈ V
